title: China Declares Computing a Public Utility, Russia's FSB Targets Global Infrastructure, and ASML Signals Chip Capacity Relief
subhead: China unveils a national computing grid to treat AI processing power like electricity, 12 nations warn that Russian hackers are systematically targeting critical infrastructure, and ASML's record quarter eases fears of an AI chip bottleneck — but chip stocks enter bear territory anyway.

sections.heading: China Makes Computing a Public Utility
sections.body:
At the 2026 World Artificial Intelligence Conference in Shanghai on Friday, Chinese officials unveiled a plan to unify the country's domestic computing resources into a single national grid, treating processing power as a basic public utility. According to the National Data Administration, China's intelligent computing capacity has reached 1,882 EFLOPS, powered by 14.45 million standard server racks.

Gao Wen, director of Peng Cheng Laboratory, outlined a three-stage roadmap: roughly 70% of computing resources have already been pooled into a unified real-time database; the second phase over the next five years will coordinate computing tasks among selected participants; and the final stage — potentially a decade or two away — would let users access computing power as seamlessly as electricity.

State-backed server manufacturer Sugon also announced the Sugon 8000, China's first fully domestically developed AI computing cluster at 100,000-accelerator scale. Sugon vice-president Wang Shengyong said the platform was built entirely on domestic technology, 'from AI processors and high-speed interconnects to system software.' Additionally, officials debuted the first satellite constellation under the Star Nexus initiative, with plans for 1,000 spacecraft for space-based 'space-data-space-compute' architectures.

The Atlantic reports that Beijing's push for self-sufficiency comes at a cost: Chinese AI firms are operating without the best chips, and Beijing has even banned domestic companies from ordering Nvidia's H200 chips, preferring local alternatives despite their inferiority. As CSIS senior adviser Scott Kennedy told The Atlantic, recreating semiconductor supply chains in one place is 'essentially impossible' and attempting it is 'cost-prohibitive and self-defeating.'
sections.whyItMatters: China is building the institutional infrastructure for sovereign compute at a scale no other country is attempting. If the grid works, it could give Chinese AI firms a structural advantage in access to compute. If it doesn't, the cost of forcing domestic alternatives will compound.

sections.heading: 12 Nations Warn of Russian FSB Targeting Critical Infrastructure
sections.body:
The UK's National Cyber Security Centre and 18 government agencies from 12 countries issued a joint advisory warning that hackers linked to the Russian Federal Security Service are systematically targeting critical infrastructure worldwide through compromised routers. The group, designated Centre 16 — also known as Berserk Bear, Dragonfly, and Static Tundra — is attributed to Russia's FSB.

The advisory was signed by agencies from Australia, Canada, the Czech Republic, Denmark, Estonia, Finland, France, Italy, New Zealand, Poland, Sweden, and the United States. The UK also joined EU member states in sanctioning 24 individuals involved in destructive cyberattacks linked to Russian intelligence, including 10 members of a sophisticated cybercrime network and multiple senior GRU officers. The sanctions specifically reference Centre 16's thwarted December 2025 attack on Poland's energy grid.

Australia's Signals Directorate separately warned that Russian hackers are 'actively targeting' critical industries including communications, energy, financial services, and defense — with hospitals and local councils especially vulnerable. Semperis director Lincoln Goldsmith described the threat as 'not a case of if, but when.'
sections.whyItMatters: This is the broadest multilateral cyber warning since Russia's invasion of Ukraine. The FSB isn't probing — it's positioning for disruption of energy, water, and health systems across NATO and allied nations. The 12-nation scope signals that collective attribution is becoming the new baseline for cyber deterrence.

sections.heading: ASML Posts Record Quarter, Raises 2026 Forecast and Plans Capacity Boost
sections.body:
ASML, the world's dominant supplier of chipmaking equipment, reported Q2 revenue of €9.3 billion with a gross margin of 54%, beating its own guidance and analyst expectations. Earnings per share of $8.68 per ADR surpassed Wall Street estimates of $7.92 by 9.6%. The company raised its 2026 annual revenue guidance to €43–€45 billion, up from the previous €36–€40 billion forecast issued in April.

The standout segment was Installed Base Management — service contracts and equipment upgrades — which generated €2.76 billion, roughly €300 million above forecasts. ASML also announced that low-NA EUV production capacity is set to grow 30% in 2027, with another potential 30% boost being evaluated for 2028.

System sales totaled €6.6 billion, with EUV equipment accounting for €3.8 billion. Logic chips represented 51% of the customer mix and Memory 49%. ASML's shares have climbed approximately 69% year-to-date and 145% over the trailing twelve months.
sections.whyItMatters: ASML's capacity expansion plan is the most concrete signal yet that the AI chip production bottleneck has a timeline for resolution. If 2027-2028 capacity targets hold, the supply constraint that has defined the GPU scramble begins to ease — though only for customers who can afford ASML's machines.

sections.heading: CXMT's $8.6 Billion IPO Tests China's Chip Ambitions Amid Global Selloff
sections.body:
Chinese DRAM maker CXMT's $8.6 billion initial public offering on Shanghai's STAR Market was more than 500 times oversubscribed by institutional investors, according to a Reuters report citing a Sunday filing. The oversubscription signals massive domestic appetite for China's chip champions — but demand was less feverish than recent Chinese tech IPOs, reflecting caution amid a global chip stock selloff.

The IPO comes at a paradoxical moment for CXMT. The company is on a US trade blacklist, yet Apple is reportedly seeking low-end chips from CXMT to address a global shortage in legacy semiconductors. As Reuters' Breakingviews noted, Apple's pursuit of a blacklisted producer underscores the global shortage in low-tech chips — the kind that CXMT, which has doubled its IPO fundraising target, is positioned to fill.

The semiconductor sector has officially entered a bear market. The PHLX Semiconductor Index (SOX) has plunged more than 20%, and the Nasdaq 100 dropped 1.5% for its worst weekly performance in nearly a month after a Chinese startup unveiled a new AI model that raised competitive concerns. US equities ended the week lower as chip stocks bore the brunt.
sections.whyItMatters: CXMT's IPO is a litmus test for China's chip sovereignty strategy: massive domestic demand, but still dependent on global market sentiment. The company's blacklisting by Washington hasn't stopped Western firms from needing its products — a sign that the bifurcation in chip supply chains is creating as many shortages as it solves.

sections.heading: South Korea Hikes Rates, Warns of 'Dutch Disease' from Semiconductor Boom
sections.body:
South Korea's central bank raised its benchmark interest rate by 25 basis points to 2.75% on July 16 — the first hike in three and a half years — and flagged more tightening ahead. The Bank of Korea cited brisk economic growth fanning inflation risks from the semiconductor boom.

More strikingly, the BOK warned that the AI-driven semiconductor surge risks creating 'Dutch disease' — where a booming export sector crowds out other economic activity. An analysis found that South Korea's real income growth rate has surpassed its GDP growth by the largest margin on record, driven by rising semiconductor prices that improved the terms of trade. A separate report in the Korea Economic Daily noted that the semiconductor boom's ripple effects on domestic demand are likely to be larger than in previous cycles.

The rate hike comes amid broader Asian monetary tightening. The Bank of Japan also raised rates to a 31-year high, signaling further hikes ahead, while the ECB held rates after its recent increase. Asian markets are trading cautiously as oil above $90 and Fed rate fears compound inflation concerns.
sections.whyItMatters: When the central bank of the world's semiconductor capital warns of Dutch disease, it's a signal that the AI chip boom is creating macro distortions. South Korea's rate hike — the first since 2023 — is a reminder that chip sovereignty has inflationary side effects at home.

sections.heading: EU Takes on Google and Apple Over AI Assistants
sections.body:
The European Union is escalating its regulatory confrontation with Google and Apple over the future of AI assistants. Both companies have a built-in distribution advantage through their control of billions of smartphones running Android and iOS — and the EU wants to force them open.

According to CNN, the EU has ordered changes to Android to boost AI competition, arguing that Google's integration of Gemini into the operating system gives it an unfair edge. The dispute centers on whether platform control of mobile operating systems constitutes a gatekeeping advantage in the emerging AI assistant market. Apple faces similar scrutiny over its integration of AI capabilities into iOS.

The moves could give competitors like OpenAI's ChatGPT a bigger edge by requiring Google and Apple to open their platforms to rival AI assistants on equal terms — or face further regulatory action.
sections.whyItMatters: The EU is betting that the AI assistant market won't be winner-take-all — and that the gatekeepers of mobile operating systems shouldn't also control the AI layer. If these rules stick, they could reshape how a billion users access AI, turning the smartphone OS into regulated infrastructure for AI competition.

sections.heading: SpaceX Targets Thursday Starship Launch as Orbital Ambitions Expand
sections.body:
SpaceX said it is targeting Thursday, July 23 for another attempt to launch its Starship rocket, according to a Reuters report. CEO Elon Musk confirmed the timeline in a post on X. The launch attempt comes as SpaceX continues to expand its orbital infrastructure ambitions, having recently debuted on the Nasdaq-100 after its IPO.

The Starship program is central to SpaceX's long-term vision of orbital data centers and deep-space logistics. China's computing grid announcement this week notably included a space-based compute component — the Star Nexus satellite constellation, targeting 1,000 spacecraft for orbital 'space-data-space-compute' architectures — underscoring that the race for orbital computing infrastructure is intensifying.

Meanwhile, SpaceX's Nasdaq-listed shares have fallen to around $124, hitting an all-time low amid broader tech market turbulence.
sections.whyItMatters: Both the US and China are now explicitly pursuing orbital compute infrastructure. SpaceX's Starship and China's Star Nexus represent different models — commercial vs. state-directed — but the convergence on space-based data processing signals that the next frontier of compute sovereignty isn't just terrestrial.

bottomLine: China took its most ambitious step yet toward sovereign compute, declaring that processing power should be as accessible as electricity — backed by a national grid, a 100,000-accelerator domestic cluster, and an orbital constellation plan. Meanwhile, 12 nations collectively named Russia's FSB as a systematic threat to global critical infrastructure, ASML eased chip supply fears with record earnings and a capacity roadmap, and South Korea's central bank became the first major chip-economy central bank to warn that the semiconductor boom itself might be destabilizing. The week closed with chip stocks in bear territory, a Chinese AI startup rattling the Nasdaq, and the unspoken question growing louder: can the AI economy keep expanding without breaking something?
sources: China Daily, The Atlantic, Reuters, NCSC UK, PublicTechnology.net, News.com.au, Blockonomi, Finbold, ShareCafe, CNN, Bank of Korea, Reuters (BOK), Korea Economic Daily, EconoTimes, IG Markets, Seeking Alpha, Moneycontrol, CNBC, Space.com
